ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper does not introduce a new algorithm or modeling method; instead it presents a systematic empirical study of transfer in reading comprehension (see ‘‘To our knowledge this work is the first study of transfer learning in reading comprehension’’) by pre-training AS Reader on BookTest/CNN/DM and testing (Sections 4.1–4.3) including analysis of embeddings vs encoders. This is a useful, moderately novel empirical perspective filling a gap (transfer analyses in RC), but it is essentially an experimental investigation using existing models and datasets rather than a non‑trivial methodological advance.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear logical flow (Introduction → Datasets → Model → Experiments → Conclusion) and states specific research questions and contributions early on (end of Introduction). However the provided text contains several presentation issues: LaTeX/artifact remnants, duplicated paragraphs, typos (“avalable”, “suprisingly”), and a badly formatted/garbled Table/appendix region that reduces the effectiveness of figures and tables. Notation is minimal and largely consistent, though the paper often defers equations to prior work (Kadlec et al. 2016b) rather than including them, which together with the formatting glitches means a bit of polishing and cleanup is needed for publication-ready clarity.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main empirical claims (poor zero-shot transfer from BookTest/CNN-DM to bAbI/SQuAD, clear benefit of pre-training when followed by small target-domain fine-tuning, and that both embeddings and encoder parameters contribute) are supported by extensive experiments (many model instances, per-task results, and p-values reported in Appendix D). Experimental methodology is generally sound: appropriate baselines (random, most-frequent, randomly-initialized models), multiple random splits, ensembles, and statistical tests; limitations (single-word SQuAD subset, cloze-conversion of bAbI, and CNN/DM anonymization) are acknowledged but do reduce external validity and complicate direct comparison between pre-training datasets. No mathematical derivations were needed; conclusions are proportional to the presented results, so only minor methodological caveats prevent a perfect score.
